narrative: Your law school application was denied because our prediction model estimated a 69.3% chance that you would not pass the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This threshold is set to prioritize admitting those most likely to succeed on the bar right away, helping the school maintain its strong outcomes. For you, the model's decision hinged most on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which stood out as the strongest signal pushing toward a failure prediction—it's well below the typical 6th decile among students who passed. Your Year 1 decile of 1st came in second, also pulling the prediction toward failure compared to the average 6th decile for successful bar takers. Interestingly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 worked in your favor, nudging the model toward expecting a pass since it's close to the 3.24 average for those who succeeded, softening the overall risk slightly. Your LSAT score of 34 and upper-middle family income aligned reasonably with passing students' profiles—LSATs around 37 and upper-middle being common at 45%—but couldn't outweigh the early decile concerns. Being full-time, like 93% of passers, was neutral here. Overall, your profile showed early academic struggles in undergrad deciles that mirrored risks for others who later failed the bar, unlike accepted applicants whose deciles typically hit mid-to-upper ranges from the start. This isn't a judgment on your potential, just a data-driven flag on bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward 37 or higher, strengthening your undergrad record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with those updates—many students in similar spots have turned it around that way. You might also reach out to admissions for feedback or explore bridge programs that support bar prep from day one. With targeted improvements, your full-time commitment and solid GPA foundation position you well for a stronger prediction next time.
